nativeRange.habitat: Temporary shallow pools and flooded grassland depressions on the Pampas. These pools dry out completely each dry season, and the fish survive only as drought-resistant eggs buried in the substrate, which hatch when rainfall returns.

decorPreferences: peat or fine dark sand substrate at least 5 cm deep for spawning
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce glare
sparse vegetation (Java moss, hornwort) as cover

diet.notes: The Argentine Pearlfish feeds predominantly on small live or frozen invertebrates. Live daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, grindal worms, and cyclops are all suitable staples, and frozen bloodworms and cyclops are often accepted as well. Dry foods are generally ignored or refused, so conditioning pairs for spawning on live foods is strongly recommended.

goodWith: small peaceful characins tolerant of cool water
other annual killifish of similar size kept in separate tanks
avoidWith: small invertebrates (shrimp, snails — will be eaten)
multiple males in small tanks (high conspecific aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Catxx; 640x407. In-water male showing the diagnostic iridescent blue-green body, rows of pearly-blue spots, and dark-bordered fins. Sharp, whole fish framed; modest resolution.
Photo by Julian G; 711x359. Clean full-body cutout of a male on white; sharp and complete but coloration darker/duller than the recommended pick, so kept as a secondary view.

summary: The Argentine Pearlfish (Austrolebias nigripinnis) is a small annual killifish from the seasonal pools of the Argentine and Uruguayan Pampas. Males display striking iridescent blue-green coloration with dark fin borders. The species completes its entire life cycle in under a year, leaving drought-resistant eggs buried in the substrate to hatch when rains return.
careNotes:
The Argentine Pearlfish (Austrolebias nigripinnis) is a South American annual killifish native to the seasonal pools of the Argentine and Uruguayan Pampas. Its entire wild life cycle is compressed into the pool’s wet season: fish hatch, mature, spawn, and die within roughly 10–12 months, leaving behind drought-resistant eggs buried in the mud substrate that re-hatch when rains return the following year.

In the aquarium, a species tank of at least 40 L is recommended for a single pair or small group. Temperature should be kept in the cool subtropical range of 16–22°C; temperatures above 24°C accelerate aging and shorten lifespan. The species is well-suited to unheated or lightly heated setups in temperate homes. Soft, acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 2–12) mirrors the soft rainwater that fills Pampas pools. A tight-fitting lid is essential as the species tends to jump.

Substrate is critical: a peat or fine dark sand bed of at least 5 cm depth allows the pair to spawn by diving into the substrate and depositing eggs. Without adequate spawning medium, reproductive success drops sharply. A separate shallow container of moist peat can be placed in the tank as a dedicated spawning site and then removed for dry storage at room temperature for 8–12 weeks to simulate the dry season before re-wetting to trigger hatching.

Males are territorial and will compete aggressively with one another; keep only one male per tank unless the aquarium is large enough to establish distinct territories. A ratio of one male to one or two females is typical. Males display iridescent blue-green coloration with dark fin borders, while females are cryptically brown, consistent with their role as egg-guarders in shallow cover.

The Argentine Pearlfish feeds primarily on live or frozen micro-invertebrates — baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro-worms, and grindal worms — and rarely accepts dry foods. The short lifespan means consistent live-food conditioning is important throughout the fish’s life to maintain peak condition and reproductive output.
breedingNotes:
The Argentine Pearlfish requires a peat or soft-sand substrate of at least 5 cm depth to breed. The pair spawns by diving side-by-side into the substrate and depositing eggs, a behavior called substrate-spawning that is characteristic of annual killifishes in the subfamily Cynolebiinae. Spawning activity tends to be most intense in the first few months of life.

After a spawning period (typically 4–6 weeks of regular observation), remove the peat containing eggs, press out excess water, and store loosely moist in a sealed plastic bag or container at 20–22°C for 8–12 weeks to simulate the dry-season diapause (the dormant resting period the eggs naturally undergo when the pool dries up). Eggs are visible as small amber spheres in the peat. After the diapause period, add soft, slightly acidic water to the peat to trigger hatching; fry usually emerge within 24–48 hours.

Fry are relatively large and will accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms from day one. They grow quickly and reach sexual maturity within 6–8 weeks. Because the species is annual, cycling new generations regularly is important — the parent fish will not survive the full year in most aquarium setups. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate, with the main challenge being correct peat diapause management rather than conditioning the adults to spawn.

primary.name: FishBase — Austrolebias nigripinnis (SpecCode 10951)
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed max size 7.0 cm TL (male/unsexed), common length 4.5 cm; temperature 18–22°C; pH 6.0–7.0; dH 5–12; distribution Lower Paraná and Uruguay River basins, South America; diet worms, crustaceans and insects; bottom-spawning with 2–4 month incubation; max reported age 1 year; IUCN Near Threatened (B2b(i,ii,iii), 2021); family Rivulidae, order Cyprinodontiformes.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Austrolebias species genus page
additional.notes: SeriouslyFish does not host a dedicated A. nigripinnis profile page; genus-level page confirmed but species-specific page returned 404. Data derived from FishBase and established killifish literature.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish species overview
additional.notes: Aquarium Co-Op does not host a dedicated annual killifish or Austrolebias care page. General killifish care norms (live food preference, peat spawning, cool water tolerance) are well-established in the killifish hobby and corroborated by FishBase ecology data.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Austrolebias nigripinnis
additional.notes: IUCN Near Threatened status (assessed 2021) cited from FishBase ecology section; reflects ongoing habitat loss from Pampas grassland drainage and agriculture affecting seasonal pool ecosystems.

fish.breedingNotes: The Argentine Pearlfish spawns by diving side-by-side into a peat or soft-sand substrate, depositing eggs that require 8–12 weeks of dry storage (diapause) before being re-wetted to trigger hatching. Fry are relatively large at emergence and will accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii from day one. Breeding is rated moderate difficulty, with the main challenge being correct peat diapause management rather than conditioning the adults to spawn.
